title: Thinking Biblically About Life: Helping Post-Abortive People
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12–29-24 - Thinking Biblically About Life 6 - Helping Post-Abortive People - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Reality of Abortion in Our Context
subpoints: 20% of all pregnancies in Washington State end in selective abortion (2022)
Nationally: 1 in 4 women will have had an abortion by age 45
Washington is the most dangerous state in the union for the unborn
This is not theoretical — we will encounter post-abortive people, and some in this congregation may be among them

outline.title: Six Components for Helping Post-Abortive People
subpoints: 1. Help them recognize guilt objectively — even if they do not feel guilty, they are objectively guilty before God of taking life unjustly
2. Offer hope — the only hope for real peace and forgiveness is in the loving, forgiving God who sent His Son to die for sinners
3. Lead them to confession and faith — without a personal relationship with Jesus Christ, there is no true freedom from guilt (Heb. 9:22)
4. Show there is forgiveness — Christ has covered all sins including abortion (Ps. 103; Micah 7:18-19; 1 John 1:9; Rom. 8:1; Isa. 1:18)
5. Help them confess to those they have sinned against — the father of the child, grandparents, others involved
6. Examine how the sin is affecting their life today — 18 common struggles (guilt, depression, anxiety, nightmares, substance abuse, self-destructive behavior, etc.)

outline.title: What Should the Church Do About Abortion?
subpoints: 1. Repent of our indifference — the Church of Jesus Christ does not treat abortion as a serious matter
Practical actions: pray; volunteer at pregnancy centers; learn biblical counseling; adopt; support pregnant women in need; give wisely; vote; call representatives
The ultimate solution is not political but the gospel — changed hearts through the proclamation of Jesus Christ

summary: In this lesson from the "Thinking Biblically About Life" series, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses how Christians should help post-abortive women and men. He opens by establishing the statistical reality of abortion in Washington State and nationally, making clear this is not a theoretical issue. He then outlines six components for helping post-abortive people: helping them recognize objective guilt before God, offering hope in Christ, leading them to confession and faith, showing the fullness of forgiveness in Scripture, guiding them to confess to those they sinned against, and helping them examine how the sin continues to affect their daily lives. The lesson lists 18 common struggles post-abortive women experience and uses 2 Samuel 11-12 and Psalm 51 to show that even the sins of adultery and murder can be covered by divine forgiveness through genuine repentance. The second half calls the church to repent of its indifference to abortion and outlines practical ways — from prayer and adoption to political engagement — to fight this evil, while insisting that the ultimate answer is the gospel of Jesus Christ transforming hearts.
transcript:
## Introduction: The Reality of Abortion

We are going to continue this series. I think there are two more lessons in it. And then we are going to start a series on what has become a popular thing — the idea of deconstructing your faith.

But today I want us to think biblically about how to help post-abortive people — men and women who have had abortions in their lives. Imagine a woman who wakes up one morning with the realization that she has murdered her own child. She experiences guilt, grief, and loss over a child who will never be replaced — the death of her own flesh and blood at her own hands. She comes to you for help and relief. What will you tell her?

This is not theoretical. In 2022, the latest year for which statistics are available, 20% of all pregnancies in Washington State were selectively terminated. The national average is that one in four women will have had an abortion by age 45. That means 19 of our women statistically will have had or will have an abortion. Washington is the most dangerous state in the union for the unborn. So we need to be prepared.

I want to propose six components in helping a post-abortive person, and then we will consider what our attitude toward abortion in general should be as Christians.

## Six Components for Helping Post-Abortive People

**First: Help them recognize guilt.** Often post-abortive people deny that the abortion was wrong and refuse to admit they experienced consequences. By educating and confronting them with the fact that they ended a living child's life, you help them recognize that they sinned against the Creator of life. This awareness of guilt does not depend on their intent or how they felt at the time. It is objectively true — they are guilty before God. They need to see themselves as having taken life unjustly. Until they recognize that, true repentance is very difficult. I do not have to use the word "murder" if another word communicates the same thing — but the reality of what happened must be named.

**Second: Offer hope.** The only hope for real peace and real forgiveness is in turning to the loving, forgiving, gracious God who sent His Son to die for sinners. Even the killing of their child will be forgiven if they repent. This is the only hope we can offer.

**Third: Lead them to confession and faith.** Without a personal relationship with the Lord Jesus Christ, there is no true freedom from guilt. The book of Hebrews 9:22 says: "According to the law, almost all things are purified with blood, and without shedding of blood there is no remission of sins." The world tries to create catharsis through other methods, but there is no true freedom from guilt for those who reject the Savior.

**Fourth: Show them there is forgiveness.** We cannot emphasize this enough. Here is a selection of passages:

- Psalm 103:8-14: "He does not treat us as our sins deserve... As far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us."
- Micah 7:18-19: "Who is a God like You, pardoning iniquity?... You will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea."
- Proverbs 28:13: "He who covers his sins will not prosper, but whoever confesses and forsakes them will have mercy."
- 1 John 1:9: "If we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness."
- 2 Corinthians 5:21: "He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God."
- Romans 8:1: "There is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus."
- Isaiah 1:18: "Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be as white as snow."

Will God forgive a lie and not a murder? Think of David in 2 Samuel 11-12 — adultery and murder, covered up — and then Psalm 51: great confession, great forgiveness. If God forgives adultery, which is a greater sin, will He not forgive murder as well? Through faith in Jesus Christ, there is forgiveness. The post-abortive Christian may not feel forgiven, but they are — and as they repent, we show them that God's promises are not based on feelings but on His written Word.

**Fifth: Help them confess to others they sinned against.** The father may have been unaware or was pressured to consent. The grandparents were sinned against. There may be a place where they need to go to specific people, confess their sin, and seek forgiveness.

**Sixth: Examine how the sin is affecting them today.** Here are 18 common ways a woman will struggle after an abortion: guilt, depression, drug abuse, alcohol abuse, other substance abuse, shame, anxiety, flashbacks to the abortion, preoccupation with death, lack of sexual intimacy, bulimia or anorexia or over-eating, another pregnancy as atonement, obsession with other children, nightmares, avoidance of children and babies, self-destructive behaviors, thoughts of suicide, anger, and numbness. These are not unusual — they are observed patterns. Start with the abortion, point them to Christ and to the seriousness of the sin and the forgiveness available, and then they will be ready to deal with these other things as well.

## What Should the Church Do About Abortion?

**First, repent.** The first thing we need to do is repent from our sin of indifference. The Church of Jesus Christ in the United States does not think this is a big deal. We may cry a little when we hear a story, but we do not treat it as the serious spiritual issue it is. If every Christian took a day off during their state legislative session and visited their representatives to speak about abortion laws — we don't even do that. Most of us do not know who our elected representatives are. Most of us do not vote. So the first thing we need to do is repent.

**Second, consider it a spiritual duty to work against this evil.** Proverbs 24:11-12: "Rescue those being taken away to death; hold back those who are stumbling to the slaughter. If you say, 'Behold, we did not know this' — does not He who keeps watch over your soul know it?" Ignorance is not bliss. Proverbs 31:8-9: "Open your mouth for the speechless, in the cause of all who are appointed to die."

Practical suggestions: pray that this evil would end; volunteer at pregnancy resource centers; learn how to counsel women; adopt — the Christian community that understands biblical adoption is the best positioned to adopt; open your home to a pregnant woman in crisis; give money wisely (be aware that some organizations like United Way direct funds toward abortion); vote; call your representative; sign petitions; share your testimony if you have walked through this.

The argument that "all you care about is controlling women" does not hold. Even if we sometimes fail to care for women in need as well as we should, that does not make the other side right.

Ultimately, the ultimate solution to this problem is the gospel of Jesus Christ. Laws cannot change the heart. If the church of Jesus Christ is excited about the gospel, living it and proclaiming it, there is nothing the governor can do, nothing any court can do. If we here commit ourselves to the discipling of the nations, there will be no demand for abortion. It all begins with Christ having preeminence over all things in our lives — in how we relate to one another, how we raise our children, how we love our spouses. Gospel living will spill out into the defeat of elective abortion at the heart level.
